item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude, nice twist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got the feeling in the beginning that this was trying to explain that unexplained air explosion in Siber over 100 years ago, but then I realized that it was much more, and far more entertaining! Even with how short it was, I really liked it and it was super satisfying. +1 :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Whoops"
I HOPE YA LIKE JAMMIN TOO!

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
To address your concerns:

  1. That's fine if you figured out the twist too early and it's also fine that you didn't enjoy the article as a result. I try to write my stuff to be accessible to a general audience, and sometimes that means not getting super weird and esoteric with my reveals.
  2. They logged it as an SCP post-hoc because it disappeared. One can safely assume that it wasn't an SCP and wasn't connected to the other two 5153 files until this happened.
  3. To be fair, this was put together quite hastily, as it was a jamcon entry. Additionally, my preferred style in general doesn't tend to waste any time. I viewed this as a simple set up, build up, and reveal, and played it as such. That didn't work for you, but that's fine.

Thanks for the read and the thoughtful comment, even if you didn't enjoy :D

I'm a bit let down, I feel that something more interesting could be done with this concept if it was executed better.

Click to see full review.
Hide

After reading the first document, knowing what the title was, I pretty much deduced how it was going to go fairly quickly. I was hoping that maybe it'd end with an endless loop that the foundation would have to keep as a secret. I was wrong, sadly, and my second prediction, that I didn't like as much, came true.

I think that it's also somewhat strange that the SCP foundation would log a seemingly non-anomalous meteor as an SCP, as they did in the third document. Yes, they would most likely recognize and respond to the threat, but I'm sure there's some other form of documentation they would use.

And each individual document feels a little rushed, a little off. Not that they need to be much longer or anything, but it feels like it was put together hastily, like it was all written and put together by one person in an attempt to really hit you with that punch at the end. I know that it obviously was written all by one person, but it shouldn't feel that way

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
It's still a fair point, I've reduced the link to a random period, just so it is possible to reread it if you missed something or want to go through it again, but honestly I'm not married to the raisa headers and might swap them out with something smaller, more minimalistic. Idk yet.

I've since gone and borrowed the offset link format from SCP-3844. I've found that their minimalism suits the tone of the article perfectly.

Really neat article, goes to show its never worth taking a risk like that.

My only critique is that at the very end, it links you back to to first article. If the database thinks the last article is some sort of glitch, then why would it send the user to the most outdated page instead of the most up to date?

EDIT: just realized i made as mistake reading the last note in the last offset oops. It still feels a little unnecessary, buuuut makes more sense than what I thought it was originally

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
<Voct> http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-5153/offset/2 this is in 1946, right?
<TheMightyMcB> Hm?
<TheMightyMcB> Yeah I know Alexandria doesn't exist per AIAD
<Voct> no
<Voct> the United States DOD Pentagram Division
<Voct> quick
<Voct> what does "DOD" stand for?
<TheMightyMcB> Department of Defense
<TheMightyMcB> I'm assuming it wasn't called that back then
<Voct> bing bing bing
<TheMightyMcB> Well I'll fix it after jamcon
<TheMightyMcB> I wrote the thing at work on my phone lmao
<Voct> The National Military Establishment was renamed the "Department of Defense" on 10 August 1949
<TheMightyMcB> 1946 has to stay cause that's when the meteor actually airbursted irl
<TheMightyMcB> So I'll just remove DOD
<Voct> change "DOD" to "National Military Establishment"
<TheMightyMcB> Well it was 1947 but you know what I mean
<Voct> yeah
<TheMightyMcB> Can you please note that in a comment because I will 100% forget this in a week and a half

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Initially, I didn't get the ending, but it did leave that unsettling, strange feeling that something went wrong. Then, I reread the piece and found a line in the previous iteration that was foreshadowing what happened. The Foundation is a bit stupid in this, but the outcome is both pretty dark and a bit comedic. +1.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
I could see about adding some "today's date" coding after the contest. Glad you enjoyed!

Post Contest Edit: I have added a "Today's Date" format to the fourth offset, just for that extra spook. Listpages apparently don't work recursively so I can't use these types of modules :c

Malice sums up my thoughts on this well. This comes close to a couple existing pieces but finds some new ground to stake for its own, though you do kind of lose the punch of, you know, being alive and reading about the thing that presumably ended the world. Maybe a thing can be done with some code to make it happen on the same day the reader is reading the article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah okay this totally got me. This is about as well-executed as this concept could be, I think, and it's a pretty good concept anyway. Solid upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, neat. Personally still headcanoning the connection, lol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: l o o p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really interesting coincidence actually. I just wanted the impact site of the not-5153 meteor to be somewhere in bumfuck Siberia. It just had to fit the general pattern of the previous iterations well enough to excuse the Foundation getting a little lazy and assuming this one would also be a dud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that is some extremely interesting fridge horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems to me that it landed in the same area 610 was discovered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, so did the meteor hit in the end?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent evocation of the "wait, what? …OH SHIT" sensation. Upvoted wholeheartedly.

item.title: ...Hey, that thing's gettin' kinda close, ain't it?
item.content:encoded:
We came to wreck everything and ruin your lives
Death and destruction follow by our side
We rest our heads upon the guilt of the world
Because we no longer feel its weight
And the sun
Doesn't shine
On a dead planet

Tactical Nuke ~ The Acacia Strain

Well, here's what I got for Jamcon this year. This was for Theme 1: Explosions. Hope yous like it!
